title: Make a Decision Using Pros and Cons
summary: List pros and cons to help make a difficult decision more clearly.
learnerPromise: You will be able to lay out the good and not-so-good sides of a choice to help you decide with more confidence.
whyItMatters: When a decision feels hard, putting the reasons on paper can quiet a worried mind and help you see the choice more clearly. It also slows you down so you do not decide purely on impulse.
realLifeUses: You are unsure whether to take a part-time job and list the benefits and drawbacks
You are deciding between two activities on the same weekend and compare them
You are considering whether to speak up in a group and weigh the possible outcomes
You are thinking about moving to a new place and use pros and cons to organize your thoughts
materialsNeeded: Paper and pen, or a notes app
A decision you are currently considering
result: A written pros and cons list for one real decision you are facing.
youWillLearn: How to name a decision clearly so you know exactly what you are deciding
How to list honest pros and cons without judging yourself
How to weigh items by importance, not just count them
How to use the list to make a choice and commit to it
miniChallenge: Think of a decision you need to make in the next few days. Draw a line down the middle of a page. Write at least three pros on one side and three cons on the other. Look at the list and circle the item that matters most to you.
steps: Write the decision as a clear yes-or-no or choice A versus choice B question
Set a timer for 5 minutes and list every reason in favor — do not censor yourself
Reset the timer and list every reason against — be honest, even if it feels uncomfortable
Review your list and mark each item as 'very important,' 'somewhat important,' or 'minor'
Make a tentative choice based on what matters most, and decide when you will act on it
completionCriteria: You wrote a pros and cons list with at least three items on each side
You identified which items matter most and made a tentative decision
commonProblems: You may feel more conflicted after listing — that means the decision is genuinely hard, not that you did it wrong
You might list only obvious items — push yourself to write at least one less obvious reason
You may want a perfect answer — pros and cons help, but they do not remove all uncertainty
tips: One strong pro or con often matters more than five weak ones
Talk your list through with a trusted person if you still feel stuck
Set a deadline for the decision so the list does not become a way to delay
